1 import type { Phase } from "../../components/presentation/types"; 2 3 // Sources (every claim grounded in existing repo content): 4 // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/preface.ts 5 // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/pillars.ts 6 // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/arc.ts 7 // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/progress.ts 8 // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/quotes.ts 9 10 export const prpPhases: Phase[] = [ 11 { 12 id: "distinction", 13 heading: "The distinction", 14 body: ( 15 <> 16

17 Purposeless efficiency is efficiency severed from any external 18 purpose, until the metric is the meaning. One concrete example: a 19 workflow that has been turned into a leaderboard, where the 20 leaderboard becomes the workflow and the worldview follows. 21

22

23 Purposeful efficiency still remembers what it is for; it optimises in 24 service of something outside itself. One concrete example: an 25 institution whose primary product remains the goods or services it 26 was built to deliver, rather than the legitimation of its own 27 continued existence. 28

29 30 ), 31 }, 32 { 33 id: "why-it-matters", 34 heading: "Why it matters", 35 body: ( 36 <> 37

38 When purposeless efficiency goes unrecognised, the optimization 39 target eats the optimized object. The first case is gamification: 40 every workflow becomes a leaderboard, the leaderboard becomes the 41 workflow, and the institution survives while the thing it was 42 supposed to do does not. 43

44

45 The second case is incumbency and the complacency that follows from 46 it. Incumbents persist not because they are right but because they 47 are wired into the substrate of decision-making. Given levers 48 disconnected from outcomes, the rational response is to stop 49 pulling — the cost of paying attention exceeds the expected return, 50 the citizen withdraws, and so, eventually, does the elite. 51

52 53 ), 54 }, 55 { 56 id: "manuscript", 57 heading: "What I'm doing about it", 58 body: ( 59 <> 60

61 I'm writing a manuscript called Purposeless Efficiency — Book I of a 62 planned five-volume series. Book I is the diagnostic volume. It 63 opens with a refusal and a confession — that I was good at producing 64 inside systems whose only consistent output was their own 65 perpetuation — and then walks five pillars in turn: corporatism, 66 gamification, incumbency, complacency, and economic revolution. 67

68

69 The remaining four volumes will treat what comes after the 70 severance: the institutions that survive it, the ones that do not, 71 and what an honest economic order might look like once we admit the 72 mistake. Working titles for Books II through V are provisional; the 73 volumes are scoped at the volume level and remain in planning, not 74 drafting. 75

76 77 ), 78 }, 79 { 80 id: "status", 81 heading: "Status & where to go", 82 body: ( 83 <> 84

85 Book I is in active writing on manuscript version four, at roughly 86 92,000 of a target 100,000 words. Books II through V remain in 87 planning. There is no public release date and no publisher 88 announcement; this page exists to make the argument legible while 89 the manuscript continues. 90

91

92 For the longer breakdown, keep reading the project page below. For 93 questions, ask the assistant on /chat. 94

1 import type { Phase } from "../../components/presentation/types"; 2 3 // Sources (every claim grounded in existing repo content): 4 // - src/content/projects/theseus/thesis.ts 5 // - src/content/projects/theseus/methodology.ts 6 // - src/content/projects/theseus/principles.ts 7 // - src/content/projects/theseus/body.ts 8 // - src/content/projects/theseus/metadata.ts 9 // - src/content/projects/theseus/noosphere-feed.ts 10 // Per 17/P04 §D: the three orders of change are NOT named individually 11 // here. The project page (16/P06) handles that depth. This deck is 12 // surface-level: orders of change, severity, fixed points. 13 14 export const thsPhases: Phase[] = [ 15 { 16 id: "puzzle", 17 heading: "The Ship of Theseus, plainly", 18 body: ( 19 <> 20

21 A ship has each of its planks replaced over time. At the end of the 22 process, every original board is gone. The question is whether it is 23 still the same ship. That is the puzzle — a thought experiment about 24 identity that has been turned over since antiquity, with no settled 25 answer. 26

27

28 The puzzle survives because the question is not really about ships. 29 It is about anything that changes while staying recognisably itself: 30 a person, a company, a body of beliefs. If you cannot say which 31 changes preserve the thing and which replace it, you cannot say what 32 the thing is. The interesting move is to stop asking whether the ship 33 is the same ship and start asking which planks, when swapped, force 34 you to admit something has changed. 35

36 37 ), 38 }, 39 { 40 id: "frame", 41 heading: "The frame I'm using", 42 body: ( 43 <> 44

45 The project's frame for identity-under-change has three parts. The 46 first is that not every change is the same change — there are 47 different orders of revision, and a system honest about identity has 48 to distinguish a tap on the hull from a plank pulled near the keel. 49 The second is severity: how much a contradiction is allowed to cost 50 is read off the structure of what depends on what, not off the heat 51 of the argument. 52

53

54 The third is fixed points — the commitments a position keeps coming 55 back to under revision, the planks that survive every pass of the 56 inspector. Identity, on this frame, is not the persistence of the 57 planks but the persistence of the constraints they have to satisfy. 58 A position changes when a fixed point falls. Until then, the ship is 59 still the ship. 60

61 62 ), 63 }, 64 { 65 id: "project", 66 heading: "What's in the project", 67 body: ( 68 <> 69

70 Theseus is the working out of that frame as a system. It extracts 71 first principles from discussion and writing, stores each with its 72 provenance, and links them into a graph of supports and tensions. A 73 second model — Noosphere — walks the graph at intervals, flagging 74 contradictions, asking refining questions, and proposing edits. The 75 thesis is plain: philosophical debate can be systematized. 76

77

78 The seed dataset is twelve principles with supports and tensions 79 wired between them, including a few load-bearing edges so the 80 severity reading has something to grade. The methodology and the 81 principles list are on the project page below, alongside worked 82 examples of how a flagged contradiction is handled by the system. 83

84 85 ), 86 }, 87 { 88 id: "status", 89 heading: "Status & where to go", 90 body: ( 91 <> 92

93 Theseus is live. The current dataset is twelve seed principles with 94 provenance and a small Noosphere feed; the next step is to replace 95 the seed set with the working corpus extracted from thesescodex.com. 96 The full breakdown — orders of change, severity, refusal cases, 97 fixed points — is on the project page below. 98

99

100 For questions, ask the assistant on /chat. 101

102 103 ), 104 }, 105 ]; 106 107 // Invariant: exactly 4 phases (per 17/P04 §B1). 108 if (thsPhases.length !== 4) { 109 throw new Error( 110 `ths.tsx: thsPhases.length must be 4 (got ${thsPhases.length})`, 111 ); 112 } 113

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' }}

jobs:

      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version-file: ".nvmrc"
          cache: npm

      - name: Install
        run: npm ci

      - name: Lint
        run: npm run lint

      - name: Typecheck
        run: npm run typecheck

      - name: Build
        run: npm run build

  e2e:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: build
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version-file: ".nvmrc", cache: npm }
      - run: npm ci
      - name: Install Playwright browsers

      - name: Build
        run: npm run build
      - name: Smoke test
        run: npm run test:e2e

import type { Phase } from "../../components/presentation/types"; // Sources (every claim grounded in existing repo content): // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/preface.ts // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/pillars.ts // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/arc.ts // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/progress.ts // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/quotes.ts export const prpPhases: Phase[] = [ { id: "distinction", heading: "The distinction", body: ( <>

Purposeless efficiency is efficiency severed from any external purpose, until the metric is the meaning. One concrete example: a workflow that has been turned into a leaderboard, where the leaderboard becomes the workflow and the worldview follows.

Purposeful efficiency still remembers what it is for; it optimises in service of something outside itself. One concrete example: an institution whose primary product remains the goods or services it was built to deliver, rather than the legitimation of its own continued existence.

), }, { id: "why-it-matters", heading: "Why it matters", body: ( <>

When purposeless efficiency goes unrecognised, the optimization target eats the optimized object. The first case is gamification: every workflow becomes a leaderboard, the leaderboard becomes the workflow, and the institution survives while the thing it was supposed to do does not.

The second case is incumbency and the complacency that follows from it. Incumbents persist not because they are right but because they are wired into the substrate of decision-making. Given levers disconnected from outcomes, the rational response is to stop pulling — the cost of paying attention exceeds the expected return, the citizen withdraws, and so, eventually, does the elite.

), }, { id: "manuscript", heading: "What I'm doing about it", body: ( <>

I'm writing a manuscript called Purposeless Efficiency — Book I of a planned five-volume series. Book I is the diagnostic volume. It opens with a refusal and a confession — that I was good at producing inside systems whose only consistent output was their own perpetuation — and then walks five pillars in turn: corporatism, gamification, incumbency, complacency, and economic revolution.

The remaining four volumes will treat what comes after the severance: the institutions that survive it, the ones that do not, and what an honest economic order might look like once we admit the mistake. Working titles for Books II through V are provisional; the volumes are scoped at the volume level and remain in planning, not drafting.

), }, { id: "status", heading: "Status & where to go", body: ( <>

Book I is in active writing on manuscript version four, at roughly 92,000 of a target 100,000 words. Books II through V remain in planning. There is no public release date and no publisher announcement; this page exists to make the argument legible while the manuscript continues.

For the longer breakdown, keep reading the project page below. For questions, ask the assistant on /chat.

import type { Phase } from "../../components/presentation/types"; // Sources (every claim grounded in existing repo content): // - src/content/projects/theseus/thesis.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/methodology.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/principles.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/body.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/metadata.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/noosphere-feed.ts // Per 17/P04 §D: the three orders of change are NOT named individually // here. The project page (16/P06) handles that depth. This deck is // surface-level: orders of change, severity, fixed points. export const thsPhases: Phase[] = [ { id: "puzzle", heading: "The Ship of Theseus, plainly", body: ( <>

A ship has each of its planks replaced over time. At the end of the process, every original board is gone. The question is whether it is still the same ship. That is the puzzle — a thought experiment about identity that has been turned over since antiquity, with no settled answer.

The puzzle survives because the question is not really about ships. It is about anything that changes while staying recognisably itself: a person, a company, a body of beliefs. If you cannot say which changes preserve the thing and which replace it, you cannot say what the thing is. The interesting move is to stop asking whether the ship is the same ship and start asking which planks, when swapped, force you to admit something has changed.

), }, { id: "frame", heading: "The frame I'm using", body: ( <>

The project's frame for identity-under-change has three parts. The first is that not every change is the same change — there are different orders of revision, and a system honest about identity has to distinguish a tap on the hull from a plank pulled near the keel. The second is severity: how much a contradiction is allowed to cost is read off the structure of what depends on what, not off the heat of the argument.

The third is fixed points — the commitments a position keeps coming back to under revision, the planks that survive every pass of the inspector. Identity, on this frame, is not the persistence of the planks but the persistence of the constraints they have to satisfy. A position changes when a fixed point falls. Until then, the ship is still the ship.

), }, { id: "project", heading: "What's in the project", body: ( <>

Theseus is the working out of that frame as a system. It extracts first principles from discussion and writing, stores each with its provenance, and links them into a graph of supports and tensions. A second model — Noosphere — walks the graph at intervals, flagging contradictions, asking refining questions, and proposing edits. The thesis is plain: philosophical debate can be systematized.

The seed dataset is twelve principles with supports and tensions wired between them, including a few load-bearing edges so the severity reading has something to grade. The methodology and the principles list are on the project page below, alongside worked examples of how a flagged contradiction is handled by the system.

), }, { id: "status", heading: "Status & where to go", body: ( <>

Theseus is live. The current dataset is twelve seed principles with provenance and a small Noosphere feed; the next step is to replace the seed set with the working corpus extracted from thesescodex.com. The full breakdown — orders of change, severity, refusal cases, fixed points — is on the project page below.

For questions, ask the assistant on /chat.

), }, ]; // Invariant: exactly 4 phases (per 17/P04 §B1). if (thsPhases.length !== 4) { throw new Error( `ths.tsx: thsPhases.length must be 4 (got ${thsPhases.length})`, ); }

Hivemind

Strategic analytical software.

Hivemind decomposes strategy questions into evaluable hypotheses, attaches weighted evidence, and aggregates probabilistic posteriors into a structured brief. The output is a structured memo with citations and confidence intervals, not a chat transcript. The bet is that decision-grade analysis benefits from explicit structure scored against a layered coherence contract.

Hivemind, briefly

phase 1 of 4

What Hivemind is

Hivemind is strategic analytical software for operators reasoning under uncertainty — founders, analysts, and decision-makers who have to commit to a course of action when the evidence is incomplete and the stakes are real.

The one-line pitch: instead of a memo full of confident-sounding prose, you get a structured argument you can defend, attack, and update — with the assumptions named and the evidence attached.

§What is Hivemind

Hivemind is strategic analytical software for operators who must reason under uncertainty. It decomposes a strategy question into a tree of evaluable sub-claims, attaches weighted evidence to each branch, and aggregates probabilistic posteriors back to the root. The output is a structured brief — citations, intervals, dissents — not a chat transcript.

The thesis is plain. Decision-grade analysis benefits from explicit structure rather than freeform prose. A memo that hides its assumptions is one nobody can argue with. A graph that lays them out is one a team can attack, defend, and update without redoing the work.

§The product

The working surface is a hypothesis tree. An operator drops a strategy question in plain prose; Hivemind parses for entities and decisions and returns an initial frame. From there the workflow runs six steps: intake, decompose, gather evidence, score, red team, render brief. Each step has its own artifact — frame, tree, ledger, posterior, critique, memo — and each is auditable independently of the others.

Capabilities currently shipping include hypothesis decomposition (CAP-01) and the evidence ledger (CAP-02), where sourced citations are weighted by provenance and recency. Scenario trees (CAP-03) and counterfactual simulation (CAP-04) are in alpha: branch probability over outcomes, hold inputs constant, vary one assumption at a time. Adversarial review (CAP-05) and the briefing render (CAP-06) are scoped and not yet shipping.

The graph compounds. The longer an operator works inside Hivemind, the harder the working knowledge is to redo elsewhere; the moat is the accumulated structure, not the model behind it.

§Why coherence matters

Hivemind sits on a coherence engine. Coherence here is not “does the text sound confident” — it is a layered, falsifiable score over a structured argument. Five layers contribute under fixed weights: contradiction (S1, 0.30), argumentation (S2, 0.20), embedding similarity (S3, 0.20), compression (S4, 0.15), and structural connectivity (S5, 0.15). Each layer returns a number a human can inspect: which proposition pairs were flagged, the size of the grounded extension, whether a fixed-point iteration found a cycle, the longest support chain.

Anti-gaming is a separate term. Template overlap, prior-corpus echo, contradiction denial, repetitive filler, and fluency-without-content are weighted and subtracted from a clamped floor. The composite is the layered score multiplied by the anti-gaming score; the brief that sounds smartest is not the brief that wins.

The product layer above this is comparative. A pitch is scored against the median coherence of incumbents in its primary domain. The interesting number is domain-relative: a pitch can be coherent in absolute terms and still under-perform the incumbents in its own market. Operators making a real bet should know which one they are looking at.

§What it isn’t

Hivemind is not a chat assistant with citations bolted on. The primitive is the hypothesis, not the message. There is no transcript to scroll; there is a graph to walk, and every node is independently rebuttable.

It is not a forecasting market. Posteriors come from evidence and structure, not from crowd betting; the surface that matters is the one a single operator can defend in writing.

It is not a single model. The coherence engine combines a contradiction-detection backend, a graph extension procedure, an embedding similarity check, a compression ratio, and a structural connectivity score. Swapping any one of those backends does not change the contract the engine makes with its caller.

§Status

Hivemind is in active development; the company is at seed stage as of 2026-04. Hypothesis decomposition, the evidence ledger, and the workflow scaffold are usable end-to-end. Scenario branching and counterfactual simulation are in alpha against a small set of operator partners. Adversarial review and the briefing render are scoped and not yet shipping. A recorded walkthrough is pending.
